coredata.dc:title: Magnetic control of spin switches in a graphene quantum dot and two-electron spin qubit
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ruijiang
Dong, Qingrui
coredata.dc:description: To get a magnetically controllable spin qubit based on carbon materials, we study the energy spectra of a triangular graphene quantum dot with two electrons occupying the degenerate shell. The total spin of the system can be either 0 or 1 and thus provides a possible realization for a spin qubit. The spin-phase diagrams are employed to show the evolution of the spin with the change of external magnetic and electric fields. The spin switches can be achieved by changing the magnetic field strength in the realistic range. In addition, from the perspective of manipulating spin qubits, we present some typical control models of spin switches. Our findings support the possibility to realize a carbon-based spin qubit controlled by an external magnetic field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Spin-phase diagram
Spin switches
Tight-binding method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Magnetic-control model without assistant electrical fields
Magnetic-control model assisted by weak electric fields
Magnetic-control model assisted by strong electric fields
Electric-control model assisted by magnetic fields

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Schematic picture of a TGQD (
N s = 8
) subjected to a perpendicular magnetic field B and placed between two gate electrodes with opposite electrostatic potentials
± U
.

Low-lying two-electron energy spectra as functions of B for different dot sizes: (a)
N s
= 13, (b)
N s
= 15 and (c)
N s
= 17. The red lines are for the S
= 0 states and the black lines are for the S
= 1 states.

Energy difference
ϵ
0
as a function of
N s
. The value
B
switch
for each
N s
is labeled if there is a spin switch.

Energy difference
ϵ
0
for
N s
= 5, 7, 9 and 11 as functions of U . Inset: Zooming into the weak- U region. Small negative
ϵ
0
are marked by open circles. Every curve is labeled by the number
N s
. For the TGQD with
N s
= 11, the mean-field approximation tends to be invalid as U increases to more than 0.75 eV [26].

Spin-phase diagrams (a) for
N s
= 7 in the weak- U region, (b) for
N s
= 17 in the weak- U region and (c) for
N s
= 7 in the strong- U region. Five control models of spin switches are marked by A–B, C–D, E–F, G–H and I–J, respectively.

head.ce:title: Magnetic control of spin switches in a graphene quantum dot and two-electron spin qubit

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To get a magnetically controllable spin qubit based on carbon materials, we study the energy spectra of a triangular graphene quantum dot with two electrons occupying the degenerate shell. The total spin of the system can be either 0 or 1 and thus provides a possible realization for a spin qubit. The spin-phase diagrams are employed to show the evolution of the spin with the change of external magnetic and electric fields. The spin switches can be achieved by changing the magnetic field strength in the realistic range. In addition, from the perspective of manipulating spin qubits, we present some typical control models of spin switches. Our findings support the possibility to realize a carbon-based spin qubit controlled by an external magnetic field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fabricating spin qubits in solid-state platforms is still a challenge due to the hyperfine interaction of the electron spin with the surrounding nuclear spins [1–3]. Therefore, it is advisable to develop spin qubits based on carbon materials, where the hyperfine interaction is weaker [4–6]. Another strategy for obtaining longer decoherence times is to use the two-electron spin instead of the single-electron spin as a spin qubit [7,8]. The spin decoherence time in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is much longer than that in traditional semiconductor quantum dots [5,9], and thus GQDs are expected to become an ideal platform for fabricating spin qubits [6].

It is an essential subtask of fabricating graphene spin qubits to develop the quantitative research on the electronic properties and magnetism of GQDs [10]. There appears a degenerate shell in the energy spectra of GQDs with zigzag edges [11]. This gives a unique opportunity for forming a strongly correlated electronic system as a function of the shell filling [12]. The states related to the degenerate shell are localized on the zigzag edges and can be influenced by edge defects [13–15], edge functionalization [16] and interactions with external structures [17,18]. The degenerate shell and the related spin can be effectively tuned by external electric fields [19–27]. In contrast, the degenerate shell is rather insensitive to the change of external magnetic fields [22,28].

In this paper, we focus on the two-electron degenerate shell of a triangular graphene quantum dot (TGQD) and our goal is to use the ground-state spin of the TGQD as a carbon-based qubit. In our previous work, the singlet–triplet spin switch was obtained by adjusting an external electric field [25] and the effect of the TGQD size on the spin switch was investigated [26]. Unlike these studies, we now attempt to use an external magnetic field instead of an electric field to control the spin switch. The magnetic-field control of the spin switch is expected to provide an opportunity for spin coherent manipulation [9]. The results show that not only the spin switch can be realized by adjusting an external magnetic field, but also the strength of the magnetic field may be arbitrarily small with the help of an external electric field.

The subject of our interest in the present work is a TGQD in a perpendicular magnetic field, as shown in Fig. 1. To assist the magnetic-field control, an in-plane electric field is applied to the TGQD by two gate electrodes with opposite electrostatic potentials. The TGQD size is characterized by the number
N s
of carbon atoms in each edge of the dot. We assume hydrogen passivation for all the edges and no edge reconstruction.

The energy spectra of a charge-neutral TGQD can be calculated by the tight-binding model [29]. In the presence of an external uniform out-of-plane magnetic field, the Peierls substitution is the standard method to consider the effect of the magnetic field within the tight-binding model [30]. The effect of an external electric field can be incorporated in the tight-binding model by adding local external electrostatic potentials into on-site energies [20]. The tight-binding Hamiltonian for the system can be expressed as
(1)
H
TB
=
∑
i σ
U
i
c
i σ
+
c
i σ
+
∑
i l σ
t
i l
e
i
ϕ
i l
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
,
where
c
i σ
+
(
c
i σ
) creates (annihilates) an electron with spin σ at site i ,
t
i l
is a hopping integral,
U
i
is the gate electrostatic potential at site i and
ϕ
i l
is a phase accumulated by an electron going from site i to l due to the magnetic field. Under symmetric gauge, the vector potential is
A =
[ − B y / 2 , B x / 2 ]
and the phase
ϕ
i l
can be calculated by
ϕ
i l
= 2 π
e
h c
∫
r
i
r
l
A ⋅ d r
.

Next, we use a mean-field Hartree–Fock method based on the tight-binding model to calculate the electron–electron interaction. Due to the large energy gap between the valence band and the degenerate shell, the closed-shell system of the valence electrons can be well described in the mean-field approximation. For the TGQD with a completely empty degenerate shell and a fully occupied valence band, the Hamiltonian can be expressed as [25,31]
(2)
H
HF
=
∑
i σ
U
i
c
i σ
+
c
i σ
+
∑
i l σ
t
i l
e
i
ϕ
i l
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
+
∑
i l σ
∑
j k
σ
′
(
ρ
j k
σ
′
−
ρ
j k
σ
′
o
)
(
〈 i j
| V |
k l 〉
−
〈 i j
| V |
l k 〉
δ
σ
σ
′
)
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
where
ρ
j k
σ
′
is the density matrix element for the TGQD,
ρ
j k
σ
′
o
is for the charge-neutral bulk graphene and
〈 i j
| V |
k l 〉
is the two-body Coulomb matrix element. The obtained Hartree–Fock quasi-particle states can be used to construct the basis set for calculating the two-electron states.

When the system deviates from charge neutrality, the interactions between electrons filled into the degenerate shell are usually significantly enhanced and the mean-field method cannot correctly captured these interactions. The exact diagonalization of the Hamiltonian matrix is the most accurate and direct way of calculating the many-body correlation effects. For the TGQD with two electrons filled in the degenerate shell, the Hamiltonian may be written as
(3)
H
ED
=
H
1
+
H
2
+
V
int
+
H
Z
,
where
H
1
(
H
2
) is the single-electron Hamiltonian with the formalism of Eq. (2),
V
int
is the interaction between the two electrons and
H
Z
is the Zeeman coupling
g
μ
B
B
S
Z
(with the g -factor g
= 2 for graphene) of the magnetic field to the spin
S
Z
. The more details for the exact-diagonalization method can be found in Ref. [24]. Since the spin S is a good quantum number, the obtained eigenstates span two independent subspaces with S
= 0 and S
= 1, respectively. The energy difference
Δ E
between the lowest spin–singlet and spin–triplet states can be directly extracted according to the obtained energy spectra. The sign of
Δ E
determines whether the ground-state spin of the system is 0 or 1.

We first observe the two-electron energy spectra of the TGQDs with different sizes as functions of the magnetic field. The energy gap between the valence band and the degenerate shell decreases as the size
N s
increases [25]. For
N s > 20
the energy gap deceases to less than 0.4 eV, which means that the mean-field approximation tends to be invalid. Thus, the considered sizes does not exceed
N s
= 20 in this paper. Within the range of the magnetic field B under investigation, the orbital energy of the degenerate shell is immune to the change of the magnetic field, which agrees with the result from the analytical tight-binding method [28]. Thus, in Eq. (3), only the last term
H
Z
can affect the energy spectra as the magnetic field changes. In addition, this means that the energy spectra are not affected by the change of the magnetic field orientation.

As the representative results, the energy spectra for the sizes
N s
= 13, 15 and 17 are shown in Fig. 2. As the magnetic field increases, the degenerate triplet levels split and the singlet levels remain unchanged. For
N s
= 13, the ground state is an S
= 1 state at B
= 0. Thus, it is impossible to obtain a spin switch by adjusting the magnetic field, as shown in Fig. 2(a). For
N s
= 15 or 17, the ground state is an S
= 0 state at B
= 0. Moreover, there appears a spin switch as the magnetic field increases, as shown in Fig. 2(b) and (c). The magnetic field strength that triggers the spin switch is denoted by
B
switch
. According to
H
Z
= g
μ
B
B
S
Z
, if there is a spin switch,
B
switch
= −
ϵ
0
/ g
μ
B
, where
ϵ
0
is the value of
Δ E
at B
= 0. The dependences of
ϵ
0
and
B
switch
on
N s
are shown in Fig. 3. For almost all sizes within the range of
4 ≤ N s ≤ 20
, the value
B
switch
is beyond of the typical magnetic field strength used in qubit operations (about
0 ∼ 6
T). Thus, the use of magnetic fields to control spin switches is greatly limited.

According to the above analysis, small negative
ϵ
0
is a prerequisite for realizing spin switch by adjusting a realistic magnetic field. The use of electric fields is an available method to meet the prerequisite [25]. As the representative results, the
ϵ
0
− U
curves for four sizes are shown in Fig. 4. The U region containing small negative
ϵ
0
can been regarded as two parts: the weak- U region (
U ≤
0.1 eV) and the strong- U region (
0 . 5 ≤ U ≤
1.0 eV). For
N s
= 5, the small negative
ϵ
0
is only in the strong- U region. For
N s
= 11, the small negative
ϵ
0
are only in the weak- U region. For
N s
= 7 or 9, the small negative
ϵ
0
are both in the weak- U region and in the strong- U region.

In the section, the spin-phase diagrams (contours of the spin) are employed to analyze the dependence of the spin switches on B and U . To demonstrate the spin switches in the weak- U region, the spin-phase diagrams for the sizes
N s
= 7 and 17 are shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b), respectively. As B and/or U increase, the ground-state spins switch from 0 finally to 1 . For different
N s
, the outlines of the boundaries between the S
= 0 and S
= 1 regions are different from each other, which can be understood by the different behaviors of the
ϵ
0
− U
curves. To demonstrate the spin switches in the strong- U region, the spin-phase diagram for
N s
= 17 is shown in Fig. 5(c). As B increase and/or U decrease, the ground-state spin switches from 0 to 1 . Since the
ϵ
0
− U
curve is almost linear within the strong- U region, the boundary between the S
= 0 and S
= 1 regions is also almost linear.

The evolution of the spins with the changes of B and U can be clearly shown by the spin-phase diagrams, which helps to analyze the control models of spin switches. In the following, from the perspective of manipulating spin qubits, we present some typical control models of spin switches related to the external magnetic field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic-control model without assistant electrical fields
ce:section.ce:para:
In the absence of an external electric field, the spin can also switch between 0 and 1 by adjusting the magnetic field B . The control model of spin switches is marked by A–B in Fig. 5. In this model, the value
B
switch
is about 49 T for
N s
= 7, which is much higher than the typical magnetic field strength used in qubit operations. Meanwhile, the value
B
switch
is about 6 T for
N s
= 17, which is realistic magnetic field strength. To qubit operations, the advantage of the control model is not to require an assistant electrical field. Although it is difficult for most of the sizes to get the required
B
switch
, the magnetic-control model is still feasible for a few specific sizes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic-control model assisted by weak electric fields
ce:section.ce:para:
With the help of a constant weaker electric field (
U ≤ 0 . 1
eV
), the spin can switch between 0 and 1 by adjusting a very weak magnetic field. The magnetic-control model assisted by a weaker electric field is marked by C–D in Fig. 5. For some of the TGQD sizes, small negative
ϵ
0
can be obtained by adjusting U within the range of
U ≤ 0 . 1
eV
, as shown in Fig. 4. Thus, in the model, the value
B
switch
may be arbitrarily small, which allows to use realistic magnetic field strength to control spin qubits. Moreover, the assistant electric field is weaker, which can reduce the interference between gate voltages and source/drain voltages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic-control model assisted by strong electric fields
ce:section.ce:para:
With the help of a constant stronger electric field (
0 . 5 ≤ U ≤ 1 . 0
eV
), the spin can also switch between 0 and 1 by adjusting a very weak magnetic field. The magnetic-control model assisted by a stronger electric field is marked by E–F in Fig. 5. Similarly to the C–D model, the value
B
switch
may also be arbitrarily small. Although the stronger assistant electric field is required, the model allows that more TGQDs with different sizes can be used for magnetic-control spin qubits, such as the TGQDs with
N s
= 5, 6 and 8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electric-control model assisted by magnetic fields
ce:section.ce:para:
In the absence of a magnetic field, the spin switch can be obtained by adjusting a weaker electric field. The control model of spin switches is marked by G–H in Fig. 5. The switch voltages for different
N s
are distributed across the region of
U ≤ 0 . 1
eV
. With the help of a constant magnetic field, the switch voltage may be arbitrarily small. The electric-control model assisted by an external magnetic field is marked by I–J in Fig. 5. The control model can avoid the interference between gate voltages and source/drain voltages. Considering the difficulty of preparing stronger assistant magnetic fields, this control model is only applicable to the TGQDs of a few sizes.

The results presented above assume perfect zigzag edges in the studied TGQD. However, in a realistic TGQD, the zigzag edges may be imperfect because of edge defects, attached functional groups or interact with external structures. Thus it is interesting to discuss the influences of the edge imperfections on the above results.

First, we discuss the influence of edge defects on the results. In a TGQD with perfect zigzag edges, the states related to the degenerate shell are localized exclusively on the sublattices to which the zigzag edges belong and thus the degenerate shell is very sensitive to the breaking of the edge-lattice symmetry. The sensitivity was confirmed in the theoretical and experimental investigations [13–15]. Therefore, we believe that, once an edge defect occurs in the studied TGQD, the edge defect can significantly influence our results.

Second, we analyze the influence of edge functionalization on the results. In graphene, the
p
z
orbitals are perpendicular to all
s
p
2
orbitals and thus are considered decoupled with the
s
p
2
orbitals. The
p
z
orbitals determine the electronic properties of graphene while the
s
p
2
orbitals are responsible for the mechanical properties of graphene. The edge atoms of graphene have only two carbon neighbors, which means that one of the three
s
p
2
bonds becomes a dangling bond. It has been found that various functional groups can evidently affect the electronic structure of a TGQD [16]. Thus we predict that, if a functional group affects the
p
z
electrons of the studied TGQD, the edge functionalization can have a significant influence on our results. In addition, hydrogen passivation used to increase the stability of GQDs has no influence on our results, since hydrogen atoms do not contribute to the
p
z
electrons of graphene [14].

Third, we think about the influences of interactions with external structures on the results. Unlike chemical-bond effects, these influences are usually much weaker, since the influences depend on the potential fields generated by the structures. Metal electrodes used as electron reservoirs and substrates supporting GQDs are the two most common types of external structures. The metal electrodes as electron reservoirs are usually assumed to be at infinity [17], which suggests that the influence of the metal electrodes on our results can be neglected. A Ni substrate can introduce Rashba spin–orbital interaction in graphene [18]. Although the energy change caused by the interaction is not significant, it can lead to mixing of spin states. Therefore, future work should pay attention to the effect of Rashba spin–orbital interaction induced by substrates on the spin switches.

To use the two-electron spin of a TGQD as a carbon-based qubit and to control the spin qubit by an external magnetic field, we have investigated the dependence of the spin switches on the external magnetic and electric fields. The magnetic control of the spin switches is affected significantly by the dot size. The effect of the magnetic field on the energy spectra is limited to the spin Zeeman energy. Thus, small negative
ϵ
0
is the prerequisite for realizing the magnetic control. The results show that the switches of the two-electron spin can be obtained by adjusting an external magnetic field. Moreover, the magnetic field strength may be arbitrarily small, which allows to be achieved in laboratories. In addition, with the help of an external magnetic field, the spin switches can also be obtained by adjusting a very weak electric field. Also we discussed the influences of imperfect zigzag edges on the results. Our findings can be helpful for making a carbon-based spin qubit in a graphene quantum dot.

Ruijiang Liu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Validation, Visualization, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Qingrui Dong: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 12174226 and 62175134).
